narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was carefully evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of first-time success on the bar exam based on academic and background factors from past students. The model estimated an 88.3% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is a very high risk threshold—far above what the school considers acceptable for admission. This led directly to the rejection of your application, as the university prioritizes admitting students predicted to pass and succeed in their legal careers. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, placing you in the bottom 10% of your peers that year, well below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack average around the 5th or 6th decile. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile also at 1, again signaling consistently low early performance compared to the average student's stronger start. While your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 provided some counterbalance—being only slightly below the dataset average of 3.21 and nudging the prediction a bit toward passing success—it wasn't enough to offset those primary academic concerns. Your LSAT score of 29, notably lower than the average of about 37, and middle family income bracket played supporting roles but weren't the top drivers here. Full-time enrollment status, common among 93% of students, didn't sway things much either. Compared to typical admitted students, who often rank in higher deciles early and throughout undergrad, your profile stood out as higher risk for bar exam struggles despite your dedication. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many from similar Asian family circumstances thrive with targeted improvements—but the model weighs these patterns heavily from historical data. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic deciles through stronger coursework or retakes, aim to retake the LSAT for a score closer to 37 or above, and consider programs offering bar prep support or part-time options if that fits your full-time work plans. With these steps, you could strengthen future applications and rewrite your trajectory toward bar exam success.
